I want to be careful here. I am not romanticising panic. There is a difference between *going towards the thing* and *running at it without thinking*. The point is not to abandon judgement. The point is that in the first ten minutes, your visibility is itself an act of leadership. You are not yet making the big decisions. You are establishing, by walking through the building, that the big decisions are going to be made by someone who is present.

## What the manuals get right, and where they stop

I do not want to be unfair to the manuals. They are there for a reason. Most of what happens in any complicated business is some variation on something that has happened before, and the manuals are the slow, patient memory of the organisation. They keep you from repeating, in a panic, the mistakes a hundred predecessors have already made. They tell you who to phone. They tell you what to log. They tell you what the regulator will need by Friday. In the first hours of any serious incident, you will lean on them, and you will be glad they exist.

What they do not do, and cannot do, is tell you what kind of crisis you are actually in.

That sounds strange when you first hear it. Surely a crisis is whatever the event was. The fire is a fire. The fraud is a fraud. The death is a death. You categorise it, you open the relevant section, and you proceed.

But every real crisis I have lived through has had at least two layers, and the one that mattered most was almost never the one written on the front of the file.

A fire is a fire. It is also, by the next morning, a question about whether your people believe you care about them more than you care about your insurer. A fraud is a fraud. It is also, by lunchtime, a question about whether the rest of the team thinks you knew. A lost client is a lost client. It is also, by the end of the week, a question about whether your remaining clients are quietly wondering if they should be next. The visible crisis sits on top of an invisible one, and the invisible one is almost always about **trust** — who has it, who is losing it, who is watching to see what you do.

The manuals handle the visible crisis. They do a competent, sometimes excellent job of it. They have almost nothing to say about the invisible one. That is where you, the manager with no script, have to earn your title.

## The decisions you make before you know enough

One of the cruellest features of a real crisis is that the most important decisions usually have to be made before you have enough information to make them properly. By the time you have enough information, the decisions have already been made for you by events. So you are forced, again and again, to act on incomplete pictures, knowing that some of what you decide today will look foolish on Friday, and that some of it will be defended, in writing, by people who were not in the room.

I do not think there is a clean technique for this. There is, however, a posture, and I have come to trust it.

The posture is this. When you cannot know enough to be right, decide on the basis of what you would still respect about yourself in five years' time. Not what is easiest. Not what is most defensible to the auditors. Not what the lawyer would prefer. Those are real considerations, and you weigh them, but they are not the spine of the decision. The spine is the question of which version of yourself you can still meet in the mirror, on a quiet Sunday morning a long time from now, when nobody is asking and nobody is grading.

> In a crisis you cannot decide your way to certainty. You can only decide your way to integrity, and hope that certainty catches up later.

That sentence sounds like a slogan when I write it. In the moment, it is not a slogan. It is a way of cutting a knot you cannot untie. You have three options in front of you, each with consequences you cannot fully see, and there is no spreadsheet that will rank them honestly. So you ask: which of these three, if I follow it through, will let me sleep? Which will let the people who trust me keep trusting me, not because they liked the outcome, but because they recognised the manner in which I chose? You pick that one. You write down, somewhere, why you picked it. And then you move.

I have made decisions in crises that turned out, commercially, to be the wrong ones. I have also made decisions that turned out to be right, in narrow financial terms, that I am not proud of. The ones I am most at peace with, twenty and thirty years on, are not always the ones that saved the most money. They are the ones in which I behaved, under pressure, the way I had told my people, in calmer weeks, that we behaved.

That is the use of culture, by the way. People talk about company culture as if it is a poster in reception. It is not. Culture is the set of things you do under pressure when nobody has time to consult the poster. If your culture does not show up at three in the morning during a crisis, you do not have a culture. You have a brochure.

## The conversation with the people on the floor

Somewhere in the first day or two of any real crisis, you will have to stand in front of the people who work for you and tell them what is happening. Not the board. Not the lawyers. Not the journalists. The people on the floor.

This is the conversation I have, over the course of my career, found hardest to get right. The board wants reassurance. The lawyers want carefulness. The people on the floor want the truth, and they want it from a face they recognise, in language they use.

I have, in my time, watched men in suits try to deliver bad news to a workshop in the language of a press release. It does not work. It does worse than not work. It convinces the room that the man speaking does not understand what has happened, or does not respect them enough to speak plainly. You can lose ten years of patiently built trust in fifteen minutes of corporate language, and you will not get it back by the end of the year, because the people in that room will tell the story, accurately, to everyone they know.

I have come to a few rules about that conversation, and I will share them here. They are not laws. They are habits.

- **Stand where they stand.** Not on a stage. Not behind a desk. If the news belongs in the workshop, deliver it in the workshop. The geometry of where you stand tells the room more than your sentences do.
- **Say what happened in the words you would use to your brother.** Not in the words your insurer would prefer. Plain nouns. Plain verbs. If the company has lost money, say *lost money*. If someone is hurt, say *hurt* and use the name if you have permission to. The dignity is in the directness.
- **Say what you do not yet know.** This is the part most managers skip, and it is the part that earns you the most credit. *I do not know yet whether* is a phrase that costs nothing to say and buys you weeks of patience from a room that would otherwise spend those weeks inventing answers for themselves.
- **Say what happens next, even if "next" is small.** *By Friday we will know X. On Monday I will be back here to tell you Y.* People can live with uncertainty if it has dates on it. They cannot live with uncertainty that has no shape.
- **Stay in the room after you have finished.** Do not announce and leave. Take the questions. Take the awkward silences. Take the one quiet man at the back who waits until the others have gone before he comes up to you with the question that actually mattered. That conversation is the one the room will remember.

If you do these things, you will not solve the crisis. You will, however, hold the building together while the crisis is being solved, and in my experience the building is at least half of what you are trying to save.

## What gets lost

I want to be honest about a thing that the management books do not often dwell on. In a real crisis, you lose things. Not always money. Not always people. But something is always taken from you, and pretending otherwise is one of the ways a manager goes hard in the wrong way over a working life.

Sometimes you lose a piece of innocence. The first time you understand, in your bones, that a person you trusted has been stealing from you, something dies that does not grow back. You can be wiser afterwards, and more useful afterwards, but you will never be as open as you were the morning before you knew. That is a real loss. Mourn it briefly, and do not pretend it did not happen.

Sometimes you lose a relationship. A peer you used to like, a director you used to respect, a client who turned out to be smaller, in a crisis, than they had pretended to be in good times. You learn things about people under pressure that you would have preferred not to know. You cannot unlearn them. You can be civil. You cannot be close in the same way again.

Sometimes you lose a version of yourself. The man who walks into the crisis is not always the man who walks out. The one who walks out has had to make decisions the one who walked in had not yet had to make, and the choices leave marks. Most of those marks are useful — they harden the parts of you that needed hardening, and soften the parts that needed softening — but they are still marks. You earned them honestly. Do not be ashamed of them, and do not pretend they are not there.

The reason I am dwelling on this is that I have watched too many managers, including a younger version of myself, try to come out of a crisis acting as if nothing has been spent. As if the cost was all paid by the balance sheet and none of it by the man at the top of the organogram. That posture impresses no one and exhausts the man performing it. There is a quiet dignity in acknowledging, at the right time and to the right people, that the year took something from you. It does not weaken your authority. It deepens it.

## What gets saved

But things are saved, too, and this is the part I most want to put on the record, because the literature of crisis is often weighted too heavily towards loss. A real crisis, well handled, also reveals the parts of a business and a man that nothing else can reveal.

You discover, in a crisis, which of your people can be trusted with the truth. You did not know before. You thought you knew. You had a list in your head of the ones you would lean on, and the list is almost always wrong in interesting ways. Some of the people you had quietly written off will turn up at six in the morning with a clear head and a useful question, and you will look at them differently for the rest of your career. Some of the ones you had built up in your mind will turn out to be smaller than you thought, and you will, gently and over time, adjust the weight you give their opinions.

You discover what your systems actually do, as opposed to what the org chart says they do. Information that should have flowed from one department to another did not. Decisions that were supposed to be made at one level had been quietly drifting up or down to another. The crisis lights up the wiring of your business like a torch in a dark cellar, and you see, suddenly, where the splices are and where the wires have been hanging loose for years. You can fix some of that afterwards. The opportunity is rare. Use it.

You discover, sometimes, what your business is *for*. That sounds grand. I do not mean it grandly. I mean that you find out, in the choices you are forced to make under pressure, which of the things you say in good times you actually mean. If you have said for years that you put safety first, the crisis will tell you whether that is true. If you have said for years that you look after your people, the crisis will tell you. If you have said for years that you deal straight with your customers, the crisis will tell you. The answers are not always comfortable. They are always useful.

And, if you are very fortunate, you discover something about yourself that you would never have learned in calmer weather. Most of us do not, on a quiet Tuesday, know what we are made of. We have not been asked. The crisis asks. The answer it gives back is not always flattering, and it is almost never the answer we expected, but it is honest, and we get to carry it for the rest of our working lives.

## The long tail

A crisis does not end when the immediate event ends. This is one of the lessons that is not, I think, properly taught in any management course I have heard of, and it cost me dearly the first time I learned it.

There is the event. There is the response. There is the cleanup. And then, when everyone is exhausted and wants the chapter to be over, there is the long tail, and the long tail is the part that requires the most discipline.

The long tail is the months — sometimes years — afterwards, in which the official story has moved on but the actual consequences are still working their way through the building. People who were affected are still affected. Trust that was bruised is still bruised. The new procedures are bedding in, or, more often, quietly being ignored because everyone is tired. The board has stopped asking. The press has stopped asking. The auditors have signed off. And it is precisely in this period that the lessons of the crisis are either embedded into the organisation forever, or quietly lost.

I have seen good companies handle the event well and then lose all the value of the lesson by neglecting the tail. The big meeting was held. The big speech was given. The big procedure was written. And then everyone went back to what they were doing in the same way they had been doing it. Eighteen months later, you can tell, by walking through the floor, that nothing was actually learned. The same gaps are still there. The same wires are still hanging.

So I learned, after my first serious crisis, to put a date in the diary for six months later, and another for a year later. Not to mark the anniversary. To sit down quietly with the few people who had been in the room and ask, in plain language: *What are we still doing because of that? What have we already let slip?* It is not a glamorous meeting. It is one of the most important meetings I have ever held. The crisis itself was forced on me. The discipline of the tail was the part I could choose.

## What only became clear in hindsight

There is one more thing I owe this chapter, because the brief I gave myself was to be honest about what only became clear in hindsight. I will give it to you in three sentences, because it does not need more.

The first thing that became clear, only afterwards, was how much of what looked like decisive leadership at the time was actually fear, well dressed. I would not have admitted that at the time. I am willing to admit it now.

The second was how much I had relied, in the moment, on a handful of habits installed in me decades earlier on a workshop floor by men I have already written about elsewhere in this book. Habits about standing where the work was. Habits about saying *I do not know* without flinching. Habits about not asking anyone to do anything I had not been seen, that morning, doing myself. I thought, in the crisis, that I was reaching for new tools. I was, in truth, reaching for the oldest ones I owned.

The third was that the day I had been most afraid of, before it happened, turned out — once it had happened and been survived — to be the day on which I most clearly became a manager in the full sense of the word. Not because I handled it perfectly. I did not. But because something in me, after that day, stopped waiting for permission to lead. The borrowed coat fitted, after that, in a way it had not fitted the morning before.

## A short list, for the man at the desk when the phone rings

If I had to compress this whole chapter into a few lines for the version of myself who was sitting at his desk on the morning that call came in, it would be these:

- A real crisis is recognised not by the loudness of the event but by the **silence that follows**. Listen for that silence. It is your cue.
- The first ten minutes are not about the plan. They are about being **seen, present, and steady**. Stand up. Say *right*. Walk towards it.
- The manuals handle the visible crisis. **You** have to handle the invisible one underneath, which is almost always about trust.
- When you cannot know enough to be right, decide your way to **integrity**, not certainty. Pick the option you can defend to yourself in five years.
- Speak to the people on the floor in the language you would use to your brother. **Plain nouns, plain verbs, named uncertainty, dated next steps.** Then stay in the room.
- Do not pretend nothing has been spent. Acknowledge, to the right people at the right time, what the year has cost. It deepens authority. It does not weaken it.
- Notice what has been **revealed** — about your people, your systems, your business — and do something with it before the room forgets.
- Diary the long tail. Six months. A year. The crisis is not over when everyone says it is over. It is over when you have either embedded the lesson or, by inattention, lost it.
- And remember, on the morning you most wish you had a manual, that you are not actually alone. You are standing on every habit any decent tradesman, foreman, or mentor ever installed in you. Reach for the **oldest tools**, not the newest ones. They are the ones that will hold.

That is the chapter. The crisis itself sits in my memory now in a particular way, the way a long illness sits in the memory of someone who has recovered: clearly, but without the heat. I would not wish it on anyone. I would not, given the choice and the impossibility of going back, trade it either. It taught me, in the space of a few weeks, what a decade of calm management could not have taught me, and it left me — bruised, more careful, slightly older around the eyes — finally able to do the job whose title I had been wearing, in those earlier years, like a borrowed coat.

The compass, I would discover later, does not point most truly on the easy days. It points most truly on the days when the map has burned and the manuals do not fit and the room is waiting, in that particular silence, for the man whose name is on the door to walk out into the corridor and begin.

existingChapters.title: Becoming Managing Director
existingChapters.summary: The moment of taking the top operational seat. Reflect on what changes, what does not, and why arriving at the destination quietly raises the bar rather than ending the climb.
existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# Becoming Managing Director

There is no ceremony for it. That is the first thing you should know. After all the years, after the apprenticeship and the ticket and the supervisor's clipboard and the office door and the long, slow climb through every box on the chart, the moment at which you become the person at the top of the chart arrives, almost always, in a manner that would disappoint a screenwriter. A letter is handed to you. Or an email lands. Or a chairman, who has known you for fifteen years, leans back in his chair and says, "Right. It is time." And then he asks how your daughter is doing at university, because he does not know what else to fill the silence with.

You sit in that silence for a moment and you wait for something to feel different. It does not. The chair you are in is the chair you sat in yesterday. The view through the window has not improved. The cup on the desk in front of you is the same cup you have been drinking from since Tuesday. Somewhere in your chest there is a tightening that you cannot quite locate, and somewhere else, lower down, there is a small, quiet voice that says, with what sounds suspiciously like fear, *now we shall see*.

I want to write this chapter honestly, because I have read too many books in which the arrival at the top of an organisation is described as a kind of summit moment, with thin air and a view and a flag. It is not like that. There is no flag. There is, instead, a long Monday morning, and a diary that has been quietly filling up while you were not looking, and a list of people who would now like to speak to you for reasons that have not yet been explained.

This is the chapter about that Monday morning, and the months that followed it. What changes when you take the top operational seat in a business. What, more usefully, does not. And why arriving at what looks from the outside like the end of the climb is, in practice, the moment at which the climb quietly raises the bar on you and steps back to see what you will do.

## The day the title arrives

I will not dramatise the day mine arrived. There was a board meeting. There were the right words said in the right order. There was a handshake that I remember as being a little firmer than it strictly needed to be, from a man I respected, who had decided, on balance, that I was the person to carry this thing forward. He did not say very much. He did not need to. The look on his face contained, in compressed form, an instruction and a warning and a small, dry blessing, and I understood all three.

I walked back to my office and closed the door, the way I had closed it on the day I first sat behind a desk with my name on it, years before. The door made the same soft click. The corridor smelled the same. The kettle in the small kitchen at the end of the floor was still, as it always was, very slightly too loud, and someone, somewhere, was on a phone call that was not going well.

I sat in my chair and I waited to feel like a managing director. I did not feel like one. I felt like a boilermaker who had, by a long and not always graceful route, ended up wearing a tie in a room that smelled of carpet. The brass plate on the door had been changed, presumably overnight, by someone whose job that apparently was. The chair I was sitting in was new — they had given me a different one, slightly higher, with a back that supported a part of me I had not realised needed supporting. That was, on the day, the only physical evidence that anything had changed. A chair. A line on a piece of paper. A handshake.

> The day the title arrives is almost never the day the job arrives. The job arrives quietly, in pieces, over the weeks that follow, and most of it is not what you were expecting.

I want to be careful, here, about a particular kind of false modesty. There is a school of writing about leadership which insists that the senior man must always present himself as humbled and uncertain on the day he is promoted, as though confidence were a kind of bad manners. That is not honest. There was, in me, on that day, a small and entirely real sense of satisfaction. Forty-odd years of work had pointed in this direction. The boy on the shop floor, the apprentice with the wrong gloves, the young man with the freshly issued ticket, the supervisor learning to discipline his former drinking partners — all of them, in some way, had been walking towards this chair. To pretend that I sat in it without any feeling at all would be a lie. I felt it. I allowed myself, for about half an hour, to feel it.

And then the phone rang, and the phone was the new phone, and the job began.

## What changes

Some things do change, and you may as well be told plainly what they are, because the books will not tell you. They will tell you, instead, about *vision* and *stewardship* and *the captain at the helm*, and you will read those words and recognise none of them in the life you are actually being asked to live. Let me describe the changes I noticed first, in the order in which I noticed them, and you can decide which of them surprise you.

The first change is that the calendar stops being yours. You had, in your previous roles, a working assumption that your time belonged to you and that the demands on it were yours to triage. That assumption goes. Your time, from the moment the title settles, belongs to the business. There will be meetings you did not arrange and could not have prevented. There will be calls from people you have never met who somehow expect to be put through to you immediately. There will be functions, evenings, breakfasts, charity boards, industry events, that arrive on your diary not because you said yes but because someone, somewhere, has decided that the managing director ought to be there. The skill is no longer to *manage your time*. The skill is to choose, ruthlessly and without apology, which of the things demanding your time will actually have you.

The second change is the quality of attention you receive when you speak. People listen differently to a managing director than they do to a senior manager. They listen more carefully and, often, less critically. A throwaway comment made in a corridor will be repeated, by lunchtime, on the other side of the building, with an authority you did not intend it to carry. A question you asked out of curiosity will be interpreted as an instruction. An observation about the colour of a wall will become, by the end of the week, a redecorating project. You learn very quickly that you have lost the right to think out loud. Whatever leaves your mouth, in any setting in which someone from the business can hear it, is now a small, weighted thing that lands harder than it used to.

The third change is the loneliness. I have read about it. I had been warned about it. I did not, until I sat in the chair, fully understand it. It is not the loneliness of having no one to talk to. There are, if anything, more people in your day than ever before. It is the loneliness of having no one above you in the company who carries your specific load. You can talk to your chairman. You can talk to your board. You can talk to a peer at another business. None of them is responsible, in the way that you are now responsible, for the daily, hour-by-hour weight of this particular organisation. When you go home at night, you carry it on your own. There is no manager above you to whom you can hand the last unsolved problem of the day with the quiet relief of saying, "Over to you." There is only you, the dog, and the dark garden, and the problem.

The fourth change, and this one I had not been warned about, is the way your relationship to bad news shifts. As a manager, bad news used to come to you and you used to take it upward. There was always someone to take it to. As managing director, bad news comes to you and stops. It does not move. It sits in your office, in your inbox, on your phone, and waits for you to decide what is to be done with it. There is no further address. You are the last desk in the building. Everything that has not been solved by someone else, eventually, becomes yours. That is, in the end, what you are paid for: to be the desk at which the unresolvable comes to rest.

The fifth change is one I did not expect, and which I think is rarely written about because it sounds a little ungrateful when said out loud. It is the way old friendships in the business shift their shape. People you had worked beside for years, men with whom you had shared bad coffee in worse weather, suddenly speak to you with a small, careful pause before each sentence. They do not mean to. They do not even know they are doing it. But the title sits between you, like a third person at the table, and although neither of you wishes to acknowledge it, it changes the temperature of the conversation. You can still go for a beer with the men you came up with. You can still laugh at the old stories. But there is a layer of the friendship that has, quietly and through nobody's fault, been closed. They cannot complain about management to you any more, because you are management. You cannot grumble about the board to them, because the board is now your responsibility. The friendships survive, in my experience, if both sides are honest about the new geometry. They do not survive at all if either side pretends nothing has changed.

The sixth change is the one I noticed last, and only because my wife pointed it out to me. The chair follows you home. I do not mean the work. The work had been following me home for years before I became managing director. I mean the chair itself — the posture of the chair, the responsibility of the chair, the small constant weight of being the person who is always, somewhere in the back of his mind, asking whether the business is all right tonight. You do not switch it off at six o'clock. You do not switch it off on a Saturday. You can be at a wedding, or in a hospital corridor, or watching your grandson kick a football into a flowerbed, and a part of you is still, quietly, listening for the phone. It is not, in itself, a bad thing. It is what you have, in some sense, signed up for. But it is real, and the people who love you will feel it before you do, and you owe them the honesty of admitting that the chair has, in this small invisible way, moved into the house with you.

## What does not change

But — and this is the part of the chapter I most want you to hear — the things that actually run a business do not change at all. Not on the day the title arrives, not in the months after, not in the years that follow. They are the same things they were when you were a charge-hand. They are the same things they were when you stood, freshly ticketed, on the floor of your first proper job. They are the same things they were when you were an apprentice, watching an old tradesman wipe his hands on a rag and explain, for the third time, why the weld you had just put down was not yet good enough.

The work still has to be done well. That does not change. The customer still has to be told the truth. That does not change. The people on the floor still have to feel that the man in the office sees them, knows their names, knows what they are paid, knows when their wives have been ill. That does not change. The numbers still have to balance, the suppliers still have to be paid on time, the apprentices still have to be taught properly, the safety procedures still have to be followed even on the day no inspector is coming. None of that changes when your title changes. If anything, it presses harder, because there is no longer anyone above you whose job it is to remember it on your behalf.

I want to say something here that I think is genuinely important, and which I have not heard said often enough by people in my position. **The trade does not leave you.** The years on the floor do not become irrelevant the moment you sit behind a larger desk. They become, in fact, the single most useful possession you bring into the room. The instinct that tells you, without being able to explain why, that a project plan is dishonest — that instinct was forged in a workshop, not in an MBA. The capacity to look at a man's hands and tell, before he opens his mouth, whether he has worked today — that came from the floor. The ability to walk into a building and feel, in the first thirty seconds, whether the place is healthy or whether something is wrong — that is not a managerial skill. That is a tradesman's nose, refined by time.

> The longer I sat in the managing director's chair, the more I leaned on the boilermaker who had been waiting, all along, inside the suit.

I do not want to overstate this. There are skills the chair demands that the trade did not teach me. Reading a board pack at the depth it deserves. Holding a conversation with a banker who has come to test whether you understand your own balance sheet. Listening to a chairman explain, in language that does not quite touch the surface of what he means, that he has concerns about a particular shareholder. Sitting through a legal opinion without flinching at the cost of it. Those skills I had to learn, and I learned them slowly and with help, and there is no shame in saying that for the first year there were rooms in which I felt like a guest. But the spine of the work, the part of the job that actually mattered when something went wrong, was always the part that the floor had taught me long before. The chair did not replace the trade. It rested on it.

## The quiet raising of the bar

When you arrive at a place that, from the outside, looks like the destination, a strange thing happens. You expect the climb to be over. The view, from this chair, ought to be the view. You have arrived. You can, surely, exhale.

Instead, almost the moment you sit down, the standard you are being held to changes. You realise this not all at once but in small encounters. A supplier who used to be content to deal with your operations manager now wants to be received by you, and is not flattered by an office, only by an undivided hour of your attention. A young man at the company social event tells you, quite openly, that he came to work for the business because of you, and you understand that the careless promise you have just made him over a glass of warm beer will be remembered, by him, for the next twenty years. A regulator writes to you, not to the company, by name. A journalist phones the switchboard and asks for you specifically. A customer who has had a quiet, polite grievance for two years now decides, in light of your appointment, that it is time to tell you about it personally.

You begin to grasp that the bar has not stopped rising. It has only moved out of sight. There is no further title to chase, but there is a continually rising expectation of the quality of person you are, in the chair, and that expectation is the thing that goes on testing you for as long as you sit in it.

This is, I think, the central confusion that newly appointed senior people have to work through, and the sooner they work through it the better. The promotion is not the prize. The promotion is the entry ticket to a longer, lonelier examination, the marking of which will be done quietly, over years, by people who will not always tell you how you are scoring. You will, on most days, not know whether you are passing. You will have to settle for behaving, as best you can, in a way that you would still respect if you could read the marking sheet.

I do not say this to discourage anyone. I say it because I would rather have known it on day one. I spent, I confess, the first six months in the chair quietly waiting to feel as though I had arrived. The feeling did not come. It was not going to come. I had been waiting for the wrong feeling. What actually comes, if you are paying attention, is a different and more useful sensation: the sense that there is now no one else whose job it is to set the standard for this particular organisation. The standard is whatever you, day by day, decide to behave as though it were. If you behave well, the organisation slowly comes to behave well. If you allow yourself a small piece of laziness on a Tuesday, the organisation will, by Friday, have allowed itself a larger one.

That is what arriving means. It does not mean you have stopped climbing. It means the rope is now tied around your waist, and the people behind you are looking up to see where you put your hand next.

## Things I tried to keep doing

I want to be practical, because the rest of this chapter would dissolve into philosophy otherwise, and I have always distrusted philosophy that cannot be acted on by Wednesday. So let me describe a handful of small disciplines I tried to hold onto in the months and years after taking the chair. None of them is original. None of them is sophisticated. All of them, I think, were what kept the seat from changing me into someone I would not have liked to meet.

The first was that I walked the floor. Every working week, without exception, I walked through the operations of the business in the clothes I was wearing that day, and I stopped and spoke to people. Not in a contrived way. Not with a clipboard. Not with a communications person hovering. I walked through, I asked how the job was going, I listened to the answer, and I went on. Sometimes I learned something useful. Sometimes I learned nothing. What mattered was that I had been seen, in person, by people who needed to know that the managing director was a man, not a rumour.

The second was that I kept answering my own phone, within reason, for as long as it was possible. I know this is unfashionable. There are people who will tell you that a managing director's time is too valuable to be intercepted by an unscreened call. I understand the argument. I never agreed with it. A small percentage of the calls I picked up directly, over the years, contained information I would not have received any other way, and the cost of the wasted ones was a price I was content to pay. There is something in the human voice on a live line that an email cannot reproduce. I did not want to lose access to it.

The third was that I went home. I know how strange that sounds in a chapter about the top job, but I mean it as a discipline. The chair will, if you let it, expand to fill every hour. There will always be one more report, one more email, one more situation that *only the managing director can handle*. Almost none of it cannot wait until morning. I made a quiet decision, early on, that the lights in the building would not be off later than mine on most evenings, and that on weekends I would, except in true emergencies, be out of the building entirely. Not because I was lazy. Because a managing director who is exhausted is a managing director who makes bad decisions, and the business cannot afford bad decisions from that chair. Rest, in the end, is not self-indulgence. It is part of the role.

The fourth was that I kept, in a drawer of my desk, a small notebook in which I wrote, in plain language, the kind of leader I had told myself I would try to be. I did not show it to anyone. I did not write in it often. Once every few weeks, when something had gone wrong or right in a way that I wanted to remember, I would open it and add a line. Then I would close it and put it back in the drawer. I think every senior person should have a document of that sort, somewhere out of sight, written for an audience of one. The chair will, over time, present you with a hundred small invitations to become someone else. The notebook is the way you remember who you said you would remain.

The fifth was that I kept one piece of the trade physically near me. In my office, on a shelf behind my desk, I had an old pair of welder's gauntlets I had used as a young man, blackened on the palms, with the leather gone hard and shiny where my grip had worn it. I did not put them on display in a glass case. I did not point at them when visitors came. They were simply there, on the shelf, where I could see them out of the corner of my eye when I was reading something I did not believe, or being told something by a consultant that did not match what I had seen with my own eyes on the floor. They were a small, quiet reminder that the world the consultant lived in and the world the gauntlets came from were not, in the end, the same world, and that of the two, I trusted the second one more. I would not insist that every managing director needs gauntlets on his shelf. I would insist that every managing director needs *something* on his shelf that he cannot lie to.

The sixth, and last of these small disciplines, was that I tried, however imperfectly, to be the same man in every room. There is a particular temptation, in the chair, to become a slightly different version of yourself depending on whom you are with. Warmer with the floor. More clipped with the board. More polished with the press. More cautious with the bankers. A small adjustment, in each case, to suit the room. I am not a saint, and I made those adjustments too, more often than I would like to admit. But I noticed, over time, that the senior people I most respected did not. They spoke the same way to the cleaner in the corridor as to the chairman in the boardroom. Not identically — the words were different — but the *posture* was the same. The same level of attention. The same willingness to be honest. The same refusal to perform. That, I came to think, was the deeper meaning of integrity. Not a set of rules. A consistency of self across rooms.

## On not believing your own organogram

There is a final thing I want to say, and it is the thing I think senior people get wrong more often than any other, including me, in the early days.

Do not believe your own organogram.

The organogram is a useful diagram. It shows you, roughly, who reports to whom, and which boxes carry which responsibilities, and which signatures are needed for which decisions. It is a piece of infrastructure. It is not a portrait of the business.

The real business runs on a different map, and that map is not drawn anywhere. It is the map of trust, of unwritten favours, of who actually picks up the phone when something is on fire, of which two people in the company can, between them, solve almost any operational problem if they are simply allowed to talk. That map is invisible from your chair, unless you make a deliberate effort to see it. The organogram will tell you that you are at the top. The real map will tell you that you are, very often, at the edge — far from where the work actually happens, far from where the relationships actually live, far from where the next problem will actually be solved.

A good managing director, in my view, spends a serious portion of his time refusing to be at the top of the organogram. He goes back down it, again and again, into the rooms and the corridors and the workshops where the unwritten map still runs the place. He keeps his name on the brass plate and his body somewhere else. The chair is where he signs things. The business is where he lives.

> The title puts you at the top of the chart. The work, if you are doing it honestly, keeps putting you back at the bottom of it.

I have known managing directors who forgot this. They sat, year after year, at the apex of the diagram and slowly came to believe the diagram was the company. They were always surprised, when something went wrong, that no one had told them. They had stopped being where telling happened. They had become the last person in the building to know anything that mattered, and the last person in the building to know anything that matters is, almost by definition, no longer running the business. The business is running them.

## What I would say to the version of me who sat down on that Monday

If I could write a short note to the man who closed the door behind him on the day he became managing director, I would not say very much. He was, I remember, not in the mood for advice. He was in the mood for the next thing on the list, because the next thing on the list was the thing his whole life had taught him to attend to.

But if he would allow me a paragraph, I would say this.

You have not arrived. You have, instead, been handed the longest and most patient examination of your life, and the people marking it will not always be kind. The chair is not a reward. It is a position. Hold it the way you held the boilermaker's torch — steady, with both hands, with respect for the heat. Do not pretend that the trade is behind you. It is not behind you. It is the only honest thing in the room, and you will need it on the worst days more than on any of the others. Do not believe the corridor when it tells you that you are now important. The corridor is wrong about almost everything. You are important only insofar as the people on the floor still trust you, and they will only go on trusting you if you remain, in some unmistakable way, one of them.

Walk the floor. Answer your own phone where you can. Go home in the evening. Keep the notebook. Refuse the organogram. And when the title comes off, as titles do, leave the chair in a better state than you found it. That, in the end, is the whole job.

You will not, on the day you stand up from it for the last time, be handed a flag either. There is no ceremony for leaving the chair, any more than there was for sitting in it. There will only be a quiet handshake, from some younger person whose name is now on the brass plate, and a long walk down a corridor that smells, as it always did, very faintly of printer toner and instant coffee.

That walk is not the end of the climb either. But that is a chapter for another time.

existingChapters.title: The Compass
existingChapters.summary: Distil the principles the journey has produced into a usable compass for readers still on the floor or in the middle: a short, plain-spoken framework for showing up, leading, and lasting. Close with a charge to the next apprentice.
existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# The Compass

There is a small brass compass somewhere in a drawer in my house, of the kind that costs almost nothing and would not survive any serious use. I do not remember who gave it to me. I think it came in a Christmas cracker, one of those plastic-pull affairs at a company lunch, and I kept it because I was the sort of person who kept things. The needle is lazy. It swings about for a long moment before it settles, and even then, if you tilt the case, it forgets itself and has to find the line again. It is not, by any honest measure, a useful instrument. You could not navigate a car park with it.

I have thought about that small, unreliable compass a great deal in recent years, because it is, in the end, a reasonable picture of what I have to offer you at the close of this book.

I cannot give you a map. The country you will walk in is not the country I walked in. The metal is different. The economy is different. The people you will lead were born into a world that I would have struggled to recognise at their age, and the world you will lead them into is not yet finished being built. A map would lie to you. Anyone who hands you one is either selling something or has not been out far enough to know better.

But a compass is a different kind of instrument. A compass does not tell you where to go. It only tells you, when you have lost your bearings, which way is which. It will not walk for you. It will not protect you from the weather. It will not promise that the path it suggests is easy, or short, or even, on a given day, the right one. It just, quietly, finds the line again when you have forgotten where the line was. That is all I am trying to leave you with here. A handful of needles that have, over forty years and change, kept finding their line when I had lost mine.

This last chapter is the smallest in the book and, I hope, the most useful. I am going to tell you, in plain language and without any varnish, the principles the journey actually produced. Not the ones I wish it had produced. Not the ones that would look good on a coffee mug. The ones that, when I was tired and frightened and wrong, kept me oriented well enough to take the next decent step.

If you are still on the floor, this chapter is for you. If you are somewhere in the middle, with a title that has grown faster than your sense of yourself, this chapter is especially for you. And if you are sitting where I sat at the end of it — in a chair you did not entirely plan to occupy, with people you are responsible for and very few people left above you who will tell you the truth — this chapter is for you too, because the compass does not stop being useful once you have a corner office. If anything, it becomes the only thing that still works.

## What a compass is not

Before I give you the needles, I want to be honest about what this is not, because the world is loud with people promising things in this space and I do not want to add to the noise.

This is not a system. I do not have seven habits, or four disciplines, or a pyramid with my surname on it. Anyone who tells you they have solved leadership has either led nothing important or stopped paying attention to what they have led. The work is too various and the people too irreducible for any system to hold for long. The most you can do is keep a few small instruments on your person that, used together, will tell you roughly where north is.

This is not a guarantee. Following these will not make you rich, or admired, or even, on a long enough timeline, secure. Some of them have cost me money. Most of them have, at one point or another, cost me sleep. They are not a hedge. They are a way of being in the work that lets you, when the year is finished, look at yourself in the bathroom mirror without flinching. That is a smaller promise than the books usually offer, and a larger one than most of them deliver.

This is not new. None of these ideas were invented by me. I learned almost every one of them from someone older, often without realising at the time that I was learning anything at all. What I can offer is not novelty. It is the slightly unusual perspective of having had to use these things in a place where the metal does not care about your tone of voice, and then having had to keep using them later, in a place where almost everyone around me had forgotten what the metal was. That doubling is, I think, the only thing about my view that is genuinely mine.

So take what is here as a small brass instrument from a drawer. Not a satellite. Not a system. A compass.

## The needles

I am going to give them to you as short, plain sentences, each followed by a few paragraphs of what I actually mean by it. I have tried to keep these in the order I learned them, which is roughly the order in which a working life tends to put them in front of you. You will not need all of them on any given day. You will need at least one of them every day.

### One. Do the work in front of you, properly.

This is the oldest needle and the one I trust most. It came from the trade and it has never let me down, in any seat I have sat in since.

Most days, in any job, do not call for vision. They call for the next thing to be done, and done properly. The weld to be laid down without porosity. The email to be answered without bluster. The conversation to be had without flinching. The report to be checked once more before it goes out. Vision is for the rare hours when the weather changes. The other ninety per cent of your working life is craft, and craft is just the next small thing, done as well as you can do it, again, and then again.

I have watched a great many careers come apart, and almost none of them came apart because the person lacked vision. They came apart because the person stopped doing the work in front of them properly. They started outsourcing the boring parts. They started signing things they had not read. They started saying yes in meetings where they should have said *let me come back to you*. They started, in small ways, being someone you could not entirely rely on.

It is very hard to recover from that, because reputation, in any trade or business, is built one small honoured promise at a time and lost one small dishonoured promise at a time, and the losing is much faster than the building.

So if you remember only one needle out of this chapter, remember this one. Do today's work, properly. Do not be heroic. Do not be visionary. Just be reliable, on the small thing, for long enough that people stop having to wonder whether you will be.

The rest of the compass is built on top of this needle. Without it, none of the others will hold their direction for you.

### Two. The metal does not lie. Listen to it.

This needle came to me through boilermaking, but it is not really about boilermaking. It is about the relationship between a person and the truth of their work.

In the trade, you have an honest teacher. A weld is good or it is not. A measurement is right or it is wrong. A bracket fits or it has to be cut and re-set. There is no boardroom in which you can talk a bad weld into being a good weld. The metal will tell you. It will tell you immediately, and it will tell you in front of witnesses, and it will keep telling you for as long as the thing you made is in service.

Most jobs, the further you go from the floor, lose this honest teacher. The signals get slower. The feedback gets political. By the time you are sitting in a director's seat, the difference between a good decision and a bad one may not show up for two years, and by then a dozen other decisions have been layered on top of it, and you can tell yourself almost any story you like about what happened. The metal has been replaced by mirrors.

The needle, then, is this: find your metal. In every role, there is something that does not lie to you, if you are willing to look at it. In production, it is the line. In sales, it is the order book and the cancellations. In finance, it is the cash position and the ageing. In people work, it is who stays, who leaves, and what they say on the way out the door. In your own life, it is your sleep, your spouse's face on a Sunday evening, and the look on the dog when you come home.

Find your metal. Read it weekly. Do not let yourself be talked out of what it is telling you by a slide deck. The further up you go, the more important this needle becomes, because the mirrors get larger and more flattering, and almost no one around you will be paid to tell you when the metal is screaming.

### Three. The title is a tool, not a verdict.

I have written a whole chapter on the office door and what it felt like to close it for the first time, and I will not repeat that here. But the principle distils to a single sentence, and it belongs on this compass.

A title is a description of where you have been placed in a system. That is all it is. It is not a measurement of your worth. It is not a promise about your competence. It is not a permanent feature of your identity. It is a job to learn, and a job to do, and one day a job to hand on. Treat it that way and it will serve you. Treat it as a verdict and it will either inflate you or hollow you out, often both.

The people I most respect, looking back across the working years, were almost without exception people whose sense of themselves did not depend on the line on the org chart. They could lose the title tomorrow and still know who they were. They could be promoted tomorrow and not become someone harder to live with. They wore the role like a uniform you put on at the start of the shift and hang up at the end. It was real while it was on. It was not them.

If you can hold that — which is harder than it sounds, especially in seasons when the role is going well and people are flattering you — you will be a steadier kind of leader than the average. You will also be much less likely to make the kind of decisions, when threatened, that come from defending a self-image rather than serving the work.

### Four. Promote the people who do the work, not the people who explain it.

I learned this one slowly and at some cost.

In any organisation past a certain size, two kinds of people start to separate out. There are the people who do the work — quietly, competently, often without much commentary — and there are the people who can explain the work in a meeting in a way that makes the room feel clever. These are not always the same people. In fact, after a certain point, they are very seldom the same people, because explaining the work brilliantly takes a particular kind of attention that, if you are spending it on the explanation, you are not spending on the work.

For most of my early management years, I rewarded the explainers more than I meant to. I did not do it on purpose. I did it because the explainers were the people I saw most. They came to my meetings. They sent me confident emails. They were articulate in a way that, on a busy week, felt like competence. Meanwhile, the doers were on the floor or at their desks, finishing the actual job, and I was, without realising it, slowly tilting the rewards of the company towards the wrong end of the room.

The cost of this is not visible for a long time. Then, one year, you look up and find that the people who built the place are leaving, and the people who remain are very good at telling you, in great detail, why the place is no longer working.

The needle is this. Whenever you are making a decision about money, title, or trust, ask yourself who actually did the work this quarter. Not who talked about it. Not who took the meeting. Not who was in the room when it was praised. *Who did it.* Promote that person, or at minimum pay them properly, or at minimum thank them in a way they will remember.

If you do this consistently, your organisation will, over years, become heavier in the right places. The explainers will leave for somewhere that values them more. Let them. The doers will stay, because they have finally found a place that sees them. That is the only durable competitive advantage I have ever observed at close range.

### Five. Absorb anxiety; do not transmit it.

I gave this needle to you in an earlier chapter, in the context of the bad years, but it belongs on the compass because it applies to every season, not only the lean ones.

A leader who transmits their anxiety downwards turns one frightened person into many. A leader who absorbs it, and converts it into the small concrete tasks of the next week, turns the same fear into work. The fear does not disappear. It just stops being the operating system of the building.

This is not stoicism, and it is not pretending. The people around you are not fools. They can tell when you are worried. The discipline is not to hide the worry, which is impossible past a certain age. The discipline is to be the place where the worry stops being a feeling and starts being a plan.

In practice it looks like this. You go home. You feel everything you feel — properly, with someone who loves you, or alone in the car, or on a long walk where the dog does not require a coherent narrative from you. You let the worry move through. Then, in the morning, you come back in and you say to your people, *here is what we know, here is what we do not know, here is what we are doing this week, and here is the next thing I will tell you, and when.*

That last bit is the part most people skip. The next thing you will tell them, and when. Anxiety in a team is mostly the absence of a clear next promise. Give them the next promise, and keep it, and then the next one. They will follow you a long way for that. Much further than they will follow you for charisma.

### Six. Keep the routine. Especially when you do not want to.

This is the most boring needle on the compass and one of the most important.

A working life past forty is, at the level of the body, mostly a long argument with sleep, meals, and movement. The argument gets louder the higher you climb, because the calendar will eat anything you do not actively defend, and the role will, if you let it, expand to fill every hour of your day and most of the night.

The routine is the equipment that keeps you in the fight. I do not mean a routine in the influencer sense, with cold plunges and breath work and a particular brand of supplements [unverified, since I have not tried any of that and would not know]. I mean the basic, unglamorous, repeated structure of getting to bed at roughly the same hour, eating roughly the same kinds of food, moving your body for roughly the same amount of time, and protecting the small rituals that mark the start and end of your working day so that there is, in fact, a start and an end.

When the year goes badly, your instinct will be to abandon the routine because the routine feels frivolous against the size of the problem. Resist. The routine is not frivolous. It is exactly what allows you, six months into the bad year, to still be standing in front of your people with something useful to offer. The leaders who do not keep the routine, in my observation, do not last. They burn very brightly for a season and then they break, and the break is usually invisible from the outside until it is very visible indeed.

Eat. Sleep. Walk. Keep one day a week that is not the work. Pay this tax even — especially — when the work seems to demand otherwise. It is not weakness. It is maintenance. The machine that does not get maintained does not get to run for long, and you are, in this season, the machine.

### Seven. Tell the truth at the level they can use it.

I have spent a working life around plain-spoken people, and one of the gifts of that has been a deep suspicion of corporate language.

There is a particular kind of speech that creeps into organisations as they grow. It is the language of *cascading*, of *strategic alignment*, of *journeys* and *spaces* and *headwinds*. None of it is exactly lying. It is, more accurately, a fog that has been let into the room because the fog is more comfortable than the weather underneath it.

The needle is to tell the truth at the level the listener can actually use. Not the press release. Not the cash position to the last cent, which will only frighten them. The shape of the situation, in language they would use to their spouse over dinner. *We have lost a large customer. We will know within four weeks whether we replace the work or whether we cut. Here is what we are doing in the meantime. Here is what I am asking of you.*

This sounds easy. It is not. It is uncomfortable, because the moment you speak plainly, you can be held to it. The fog has the virtue, for the people generating it, of being unfalsifiable. Plain speech is not. Plain speech makes a promise.

But plain speech is also the only kind of speech that actually transfers information through an organisation. Fog gets reinterpreted at every level until, by the time it reaches the floor, it bears no relation to what was decided. Plain speech survives the journey. It also, over years, builds a particular kind of trust that no amount of polish can buy. People will follow a plain-spoken leader through a great deal of trouble, because they at least know what kind of trouble it is.

Speak plainly. Especially upwards, where the fog is thickest. Especially downwards, where the fog is most expensive.

### Eight. Remember what it felt like to be on the other side of your own desk.

I have said this elsewhere and I will say it again here, because it is the single most important habit of mind for anyone who has come up from the floor.

You were treated, at some point, as a number. You remember exactly what that did to your willingness to go the extra mile that day. You remember the manager in the clean shirt who did not know your name, and you remember the one who did, and you remember the difference. That memory is the most valuable management training you will ever receive, and it cost you nothing because it was paid for in advance by the years you spent earning it.

Do not lose it.

You will be tempted to lose it. The further you get from the floor, the more your social world fills up with people who never stood on one. They are not bad people. They are simply people whose data set does not include the experience of being managed badly from a distance. They will, in good faith, propose policies that look fine on paper and feel like punishment in practice. If you have kept the memory, you will catch these in time. If you have lost it, you will sign them, and a hundred small dignities will go out of the building over your signature, and you will not feel it because you are no longer the one paying the cost.

Keep the memory. Visit it deliberately. Walk the floor. Eat in the staff canteen sometimes, not as a gesture, but as a regular fact. Pick up the phone yourself occasionally. Park further from the building than you need to. None of this is theatre. It is calibration. You are keeping the instrument honest.

### Nine. Build people who can do without you.

I came to this needle late, and I almost missed it.

For most of my working life, I quietly believed that the highest form of management was indispensability. To be the one who, if you stepped out for a week, the place would feel it. To be the person whose judgement was the final word, whose presence in the meeting changed the room, whose absence was a problem. I do not think I ever said this out loud. I am not sure I would have admitted to thinking it. But it was, looking back, the operating assumption underneath a great deal of how I worked.

It is wrong. And it is, more importantly, a betrayal of the role.

The job of a leader, properly understood, is not to be indispensable. It is to be the kind of leader whose absence is no longer a problem, because the people you have built can carry the work without you. If you cannot take a month off and have the place run well, you have not built a team. You have built a dependency. And dependencies, when their object retires or falls ill or simply gets bored, collapse the place they were meant to be holding up.

The needle, then, is to spend a portion of every working week deliberately making yourself less necessary. Train your successor while you are still in the seat. Document the decisions only you know how to make. Push down the calls that only you have been making, even when it is faster to make them yourself, because faster is not the metric. The metric is *what happens here if I am hit by a bus.* If the answer is *the place falls over*, you have not done your job, however brilliantly you have done your job.

The leaders I have admired most, looking back, were the ones whose departure barely caused a tremor. Not because they were unimportant. Because they had spent their years pouring their importance into other people, so that when they left, the importance was already distributed, and the place kept running on the strength of it. That is, in the end, the only legacy worth chasing.

### Ten. Pay back what you borrowed.

This is the last needle, and the one I want you to leave the book with.

No one gets where I have got without borrowing. I borrowed time from my family on every late shift. I borrowed patience from older tradesmen who could have closed the door on the awkward apprentice and did not. I borrowed chances from foremen and managers who put their name next to mine when I had not yet earned it. I borrowed years of training from a country, an industry, and a handful of companies that fed and housed me while I was learning to be useful.

You will borrow too. Every career is, in this sense, a long loan, drawn in small instalments from the people and places around you. The interesting question is not whether you are borrowing. You are. The interesting question is what you do about it later.

The honourable answer is that you pay it back. Not symbolically. In the actual currency in which it was lent. If older tradesmen took a chance on you, take a chance on a younger one now. If a foreman put his reputation next to yours, put yours next to someone else's. If a company paid for your apprenticeship while you were learning, train the next apprentice properly when it is your turn to sign the cheque. If your family carried the cost of your career, carry some of theirs now, with whatever you have left of your time and your attention.

This is not a sentimental point. It is a structural one. The whole arrangement that allowed me to walk from the shop floor to the managing director's office only worked because each generation, more or less, paid back what it had borrowed from the one before. Where that chain breaks, the route I walked closes behind me. The next apprentice does not get the chances I got, because the older tradesmen no longer have the time, or the company no longer has the patience, or the country no longer has the apprenticeship system at all [unverified, in any specific policy sense — but anyone who has been near industrial training in the past two decades knows what I am pointing at].

I do not want that route to close. I have written this book partly because I do not want it to close. So this last needle is not only for you. It is, in a way, the asking of a small favour.

Pay back what you borrowed. Then a little more, if you can.

## Using the compass

I have given you ten needles. That is too many for a working morning. No one consults a checklist before answering an email. So let me tell you, plainly, how I actually use this thing.

Most days, I use one of them. The one that the day in front of me happens to need. If I am about to make a hiring decision, I lean on the one about doers versus explainers. If I am walking into a frightened meeting, I lean on absorbing anxiety. If I am tired and tempted to skip the gym, I lean on keep the routine. If I am tempted to soften bad news into language no one can act on, I lean on tell the truth at the level they can use it.

On the rarer days when the weather changes — a real crisis, a real opportunity, a real decision about the next ten years — I sit with all ten. Not in a list. In a quiet half-hour, usually early in the morning, with a cup of coffee and the door closed, the way I learned to close the door deliberately in the office where I first sat as a manager. I ask, in turn, what each needle is telling me about the situation in front of me. Not all of them will speak. Most of them will just point in the same direction, which is itself a kind of answer. The one or two that disagree are the ones to listen to most carefully, because disagreement among honest instruments is information.

If I cannot find a settled direction even after all of that, I do the most uncomfortable thing on the compass, which is to wait. Not forever. Not as an evasion. But until at least one more piece of metal has told me something true. Almost every bad decision I have ever made, looking back, was made before the metal had finished speaking. The few good ones I am proud of were made after I had been patient enough to listen.

That is the whole technique. There is no more to it than that.

## A short word about the chair you have not yet sat in

I want to say one thing to the reader who is not yet anywhere near a director's seat, because I do not want this last chapter to read as if it were only for people who already have one.

If you are still on the floor, or in the middle, or at the start of supervising your first crew, you may be wondering whether any of this compass is for you yet. It is. All of it. None of these needles wait for a title to switch on. The apprentice who does the work in front of him properly is already practising the first needle. The young supervisor who listens to the metal — the actual production line, the actual customer complaints, the actual look on the team's faces — is already practising the second. The charge-hand who tells the truth at the level his crew can use is already practising the seventh. You do not need to wait to be promoted to live this way. In fact, the people I have seen rise furthest are the ones who started living this way long before anyone in authority noticed, simply because it was the right way to be in the work.

The title, when it comes, will only amplify what is already there. If you have built the habits, the title will land on prepared ground and the role will fit you in a way that surprises you. If you have not, no amount of training course or executive coaching [unverified — I am sceptical of most of it, but I have not been through a great deal of it personally] will install in two weekends what should have been installed over twenty years.

So start now. Wherever you are. The compass works the same in a fitter's bay as it does behind a desk. The needles do not care about your job title. They only care whether you are willing to look at them when the day gets confusing.

## A charge to the next apprentice

I want to finish the book where I started it, which was on a shop floor at six o'clock in the morning, with a young man who had no real plan beyond the next shift.

I do not know who you are, but you are out there. You are standing in some equivalent of that shed right now — perhaps in a real workshop, perhaps in a warehouse, perhaps in a call centre, perhaps in a kitchen, perhaps in a place I would not even recognise as work — and you are wondering whether anything you do over the next forty years is going to add up to anything in particular.

I am telling you, from this end of the walk, that it can.

Not by accident. Not by luck, although you will need some of that and most of us have had more of it than we admit. It will add up because you keep doing the work in front of you properly, for long enough, and because you let the metal teach you, and because you remember what it felt like to be on the other side of your own desk by the time it is your desk, and because you pay back what you borrow, and because, on enough of the hard days, you keep showing up.

That is the whole of it. There is no other secret. If there were, I promise you, someone would have sold it to me by now [unverified, but the offers have certainly arrived in the post often enough].

The compass I have given you is small and brass and slightly unreliable, like the one in my drawer. It will not navigate for you. It will only point. You will still have to do the walking, in weather I cannot predict, towards a horizon I am not going to live to see.

But you have everything you need. You have hands that can be taught. You have a back that, at your age, will recover from almost anything. You have time, which is the currency none of us spends well early on and all of us want more of later. You have the freedom, every morning, to choose how you are going to be in the work that day, regardless of what the work is.

Use it.

And when, in some year I will not be here to see, you find yourself in a seat you did not entirely plan to occupy, with people you are responsible for and very few people left above you who will tell you the truth, I want you to do one last thing.

Open a drawer. Find whatever small, ordinary, slightly unreliable instrument you have kept by you on the walk. Put it on the desk where you can see it. Sit in the chair. Close the door, deliberately, the way a tool is closed.

And start again. With the next small thing in front of you. Properly.

That has been the whole of my working life. It is, I now believe, the whole of the lesson.

Welcome to the trade.
